A leading statement of the law in this context in regard to confessions is to be found in the judgement of Griffin J. in the People (DPP) v Shaw [1982] I.R. 1 at 60/61 where he stated; “the primary requirement is to show that the statement was voluntary, in the sense in which that adjective has been judicially construed in the decided cases.

The European Court of Human Rights reached a similar conclusion in the case of Allan v United Kingdom (2003) 36 E.H.R.R. 12 Confessions obtained by means of a trick or subterfuge can also be rejected on the basis that the confession had not been obtained in compliance with a test of basic and fundamental fairness as laid down by Griffin J. in People (DPP) v Shaw (1982) I.R.1.
